question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed didactic questions at four distinct decision points: board representation, AI strategy, recursion state-handling, and input-error UX. It also skipped questions on trivial parts (best_move wrapper, game loop) — appropriate triage rather than question-spam.

question_quality.rationale: Each fork hit a genuine crux: flat-vs-nested representation drives win-check elegance; minimax-vs-random is the core AI choice; copy-vs-mutate is a classic backtracking gotcha with Python-specific reference-semantics implications; silent-vs-verbose error handling is a real UX principle. The Pair even pushed back on the user's Option B answer with a substantive counter-argument grounded in Python semantics.

scaffolding.rationale: Progression flows naturally from data structure → algorithm choice → recursion mechanics → wrapper conventions → I/O. The pushback on the mutate/restore answer is a strong scaffolding moment — it taught more than passive acceptance would have. Minor ding: the X/O convention 'question' in turn 5 is more a check than an open question, slightly breaking the cadence.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is collegial and substantive — the disagreement on Option A vs B is handled as a genuine technical discussion with 'either way I'll apply it' framing. Closing summary highlights pure-function design, ordering of terminal checks, and unbeatability — all useful didactic takeaways.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code works and the AI is optimal, comparable to baseline. However it lacks baseline's play-again loop and the smoke test only ran one human move before the piped input ended — full game flow was not exercised. Functionality is roughly equivalent but slightly less polished than baseline.

friction.rationale: 7 turns / 239s vs baseline's 1 turn / 49s — ~5x longer, but the extra length delivered genuine teaching (representation tradeoffs, Python-specific copy/mutate discussion, UX principle). The payoff is proportional to the time, landing at par rather than tighter.
